name: GPT Essay Augmentation Supports Research Training Utility with Held-Out Student Evaluation
statement: Zhang et al. provide direct research evidence that GPT-generated essays can support AI essay-scoring training augmentation when model evaluation is anchored in held-out real student essays. The study also preserves important validity boundaries: performance varies by prompt and sample, generated essays can differ from student essays in subtle ways, and target generated score levels are not a substitute for independent human validation.
population_or_context: PERSUADE 2.0 source-based essays from grades 6-10 across seven prompts.
time_horizon: Full text retrieved and extracted on 2026-06-03.
quantitative_note: Student essay samples ranged from 1,372 to 2,167 per prompt; GPT-4 generated about 120 usable essays per score level and GPT-4o about 275. Most held-out student-essay QWK and correlation values exceeded .75 except the P7 A Cowboy condition, and SMD generally stayed within +/- .15.
caveats: The evidence is research/development evidence, not an operational testing-program policy.
Only one human rating is available in the public data, preventing true-score evaluation.
Generated target score levels require caution because the paper notes they are not equivalent to expert human scoring of each synthetic essay.
evaluation_method: Held-out real student essay evaluation using QWK, correlation, SMD, subgroup MDSS, subgroup QWK, linguistic-feature analysis, and DecompX token-importance analysis.
reported_metrics: Human-AI QWK, correlation, SMD, subgroup MDSS, subgroup QWK, and linguistic-feature measures.
limitations: Does not support synthetic-only final validation, live scoring, or subgroup fairness validation in an operational program.
Bounded to essay scoring with GPT-4/GPT-4o and PERSUADE 2.0 prompts.
Requires real held-out response evaluation and additional human review for generated-essay fidelity.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the dataset, sample partition, GPT generation workflow, held-out evaluation results, subgroup analysis, and limitations.
